title: Golden Transcript Paper
abstract: We propose a curriculum distillation method ordered by teacher retrieval confidence. A lightweight consistency head penalizes students that drift from cited evidence. Across seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks, the 1.3B student matches the 13B teacher on five tasks at 12x lower

manuscript.text: We propose a curriculum distillation method ordered by teacher retrieval confidence. A lightweight consistency head penalizes students that drift from cited evidence. Across seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks, the 1.3B student matches the 13B teacher on five tasks at 12x lower serving cost. Results use a single seed. Ablations attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering.

reviews.summary: The paper proposes a curriculum distillation method ordered by teacher retrieval confidence, plus a consistency head penalizing drift from cited evidence. A 1.3B student reportedly matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks at 12x lower serving cost, with ablations attributing gains to confidence ordering.
reviews.body:
[Summary Of The Paper]
The paper proposes a curriculum distillation method in which training examples are ordered by teacher retrieval confidence. It adds a lightweight consistency head that penalizes the student when it drifts from cited evidence. The authors report that a 1.3B student matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks while incurring 12x lower serving cost. Ablations are said to attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering. Results are reported using a single seed.

[Strength And Weaknesses]
Strengths: The core idea is intuitively appealing. Ordering distillation examples by teacher retrieval confidence is a sensible curriculum signal, and coupling it with a consistency head that penalizes drift from cited evidence addresses a real problem in retrieval-augmented distillation. The reported 12x serving-cost reduction, if substantiated, would be practically significant, and the ablation framing (attributing gains to confidence ordering) is the right investigative direction.

Weaknesses: The submission is essentially abstract-length and contains no method details, no benchmark names, no baselines, and no related-work positioning. The manuscript states that examples are 'ordered by teacher retrieval confidence' and that a 'consistency head penalizes students that drift from cited evidence,' but never explains how confidence is computed, how the curriculum is scheduled, or how the consistency head is implemented. Curriculum distillation and confidence-ordered training are established ideas, so the claimed novelty cannot be assessed without these details and without comparison to existing curriculum/distillation and retrieval-augmented distillation baselines. A full manuscript must supply all of this.

Second, the central empirical claim rests on a single seed. The paper explicitly notes 'Results use a single seed,' yet the headline claim is that a 1.3B student 'matches' a 13B teacher on five tasks. A matching claim requires variance across multiple seeds, statistical significance testing, and the actual per-benchmark numbers. Without result tables, baselines, and a precise definition of what 'match' means (e.g., within some tolerance, statistically indistinguishable), the headline claim is unsupported and could easily reflect seed noise.

Finally, the '12x lower serving cost' figure is undefined. It is unclear whether this accounts for the retrieval and teacher-confidence computation and the consistency head at inference, or whether it reflects only student parameter count. This ambiguity directly affects the credibility of the practical contribution.

[Questions]
1. How exactly is teacher retrieval confidence computed, how is the curriculum scheduled, and how is the consistency head implemented? Please also position the method against existing curriculum/distillation and retrieval-augmented distillation baselines.
2. Can you provide per-benchmark numbers with multiple seeds, variance, and significance tests, along with a precise definition of 'match', so the matching claim can be evaluated?
3. How is 'serving cost' measured for the 12x figure - does it include retrieval, teacher-confidence computation, and the consistency head at inference, or only student parameter count? Please clarify what is included.
strengths: Addresses a practical and relevant problem: cost-efficient distillation for knowledge-intensive tasks with evidence grounding.
Reports a concrete and appealing headline result (1.3B matching 13B on five tasks at 12x lower cost) and includes an ablation isolating the confidence-ordering component.

reviews.summary: An extended abstract proposing curriculum distillation ordered by teacher retrieval confidence, with a consistency head penalizing drift from cited evidence; a 1.3B student reportedly matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks at 12x lower serving cost.
reviews.body:
[Summary Of The Paper]
This extended abstract proposes a curriculum distillation method in which training examples are ordered by the teacher's retrieval confidence. A lightweight consistency head is introduced to penalize the student when it drifts from cited evidence. The authors report that a 1.3B student matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks while achieving 12x lower serving cost. Ablations reportedly attribute most of the gain to the confidence-based ordering. The submission is at the abstract stage, with results drawn from a single seed.

[Strength And Weaknesses]
Strengths: The core idea of ordering distillation examples by teacher retrieval confidence is intuitive and potentially useful for knowledge-intensive tasks, and pairing it with a consistency head that anchors the student to cited evidence is a sensible way to reduce hallucination. The headline efficiency claim (a 1.3B student approaching a 13B teacher at 12x lower serving cost) would be impactful if substantiated, and the ablation focus on the ordering component is the right thing to study.

Weaknesses: (1) The evaluation rests on a single seed, which the authors state directly ("Results use a single seed"). Distillation gains are notoriously seed-sensitive, and the claim that the student "matches the 13B teacher on five tasks" is meaningless without variance. A full manuscript must report mean and standard deviation over multiple seeds and, ideally, significance tests for the teacher-student comparison; otherwise it is impossible to know whether five-of-seven is a stable result or seed noise. (2) No baseline distillation methods are named anywhere in the text. To establish that confidence ordering is what matters, the paper must compare against standard KD, a random-order curriculum, difficulty-based curricula, and a retrieval-augmented student without the consistency head. The current ablation only isolates the ordering internally; without external baselines the contribution cannot be assessed relative to prior work. (3) Key definitions are missing. The abstract claims "12x lower serving cost" without specifying whether this is throughput, latency, hardware, or batch size, and it never gives the equation for how the consistency head's penalty is computed against "cited evidence." No dataset names, metrics, or formulas are provided, so the claims are not reproducible.

[Questions]
1. How exactly is "serving cost" measured (throughput, latency, hardware configuration, batch size), and against which reference point is the 12x reduction computed?
2. What is the precise formulation of the consistency head's penalty relative to cited evidence, and what are the seven benchmarks and their metrics?
3. Can you provide multi-seed results with mean/standard deviation and significance tests, along with comparisons to standard KD, random-order and difficulty-based curricula, and a retrieval-augmented student without the consistency head?
strengths: Addresses a practically important problem: cheaper serving via distillation on knowledge-intensive tasks with an explicit evidence-grounding mechanism.
Confidence-ordered curriculum is an intuitive and testable idea, and the abstract already reports an ablation isolating the ordering as the main gain driver.
Concrete efficiency claim (12x lower serving cost) grounds the practical value.

reviews.summary: An abstract-only submission proposing curriculum distillation ordered by teacher retrieval confidence, plus a consistency head penalizing drift from cited evidence. Claims a 1.3B student matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive tasks at 12x lower serving cost, with single-seed results.
reviews.body:
[Summary Of The Paper]
This submission proposes a curriculum distillation method in which training examples are ordered by the teacher's retrieval confidence, coupled with a lightweight "consistency head" that penalizes students whose outputs drift from cited evidence. The central empirical claim is that a 1.3B-parameter student matches a 13B-parameter teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks while offering 12x lower serving cost. The authors report that results use a single seed, and that ablations attribute most of the gain to the confidence-based ordering. As presented, the submission consists solely of an abstract-length description with no accompanying method, experimental, or results sections.

[Strength And Weaknesses]
Strengths: The core idea is intuitively appealing. Ordering distillation examples by teacher retrieval confidence is a sensible instantiation of curriculum learning, and adding a consistency head that penalizes drift from cited evidence targets a genuine failure mode in knowledge-intensive generation. The claimed outcome, a 1.3B student approaching a 13B teacher at 12x lower serving cost, would be practically valuable if substantiated.

Weaknesses: The overriding problem is that this is an abstract only, not a full manuscript. There is no method section, no formal description of the curriculum ordering or the consistency head, no experimental setup, no list of the seven benchmarks, and no tables. The text asserts "Across seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks" and "Ablations attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering," but none of these are actually described or reported. A full submission must add precise method formulation, the seven benchmarks named, baselines, training details, and the actual results and ablation numbers. Without these, the claims cannot be evaluated at all.

Second, the experimental basis is inadequate even as stated. The paper concedes "Results use a single seed," and the claim that the student "matches the 13B teacher on five tasks" is asserted without any numbers, variance, or a definition of "matches" (within what margin?). Single-seed results are unreliable for parity claims, and "matches" is undefined. The authors should report multi-seed results with confidence intervals and specify the metric and threshold used to claim parity with the teacher.

[Questions]
1. How is "teacher retrieval confidence" computed? Please give the precise definition and how it is used to order the curriculum.
2. How is the "12x lower serving cost" measured, in terms of parameters, latency, throughput, and hardware? These central quantitative claims need definitions and a measurement protocol in the body.
3. What is the definition of "matches" for the five-of-seven claim, i.e., within what margin, on which metric, and with what across-seed variance?
strengths: Clear, concrete framing of the core idea (curriculum ordering by retrieval confidence plus a consistency head).
Reports a tangible efficiency-quality result (1.3B matching 13B on five of seven tasks at 12x lower cost) and includes an ablation attributing gains to the confidence ordering.

Curriculum distillation and confidence-ordered training are established ideas; the novelty of 'ordering by teacher retrieval confidence' cannot be assessed without describing how confidence is computed, how the curriculum is scheduled, and how the consistency head is implemented. A full manuscript must add these details plus a comparison to existing curriculum/distillation and retrieval-augmented distillation baselines.

Results use a single seed, which undermines the central claim that a 1.3B student 'matches' a 13B teacher on five tasks. Matching claims require variance across multiple seeds, statistical significance, and the actual numbers per benchmark. Without tables, baselines, and defined metrics for 'match', the headline claim is unsupported.

How is 'serving cost' measured for the 12x figure - does it account for the retrieval/teacher-confidence computation and the consistency head at inference, or only student parameter count? Please clarify what is included.

Results use a single seed by the authors' own admission. Distillation gains are notoriously seed-sensitive, and 'matches on five of seven tasks' is meaningless without variance. A full manuscript must report mean and standard deviation over multiple seeds and, ideally, significance tests for the teacher-student comparison.

No baseline distillation methods are named. To establish that confidence ordering matters, you must compare against standard KD, random-order curriculum, and difficulty-based curricula, plus a retrieval-augmented student without the consistency head. Without these the contribution cannot be assessed.

How is 'serving cost' measured (throughput, latency, hardware, batch size), and how is the consistency head's penalty computed against 'cited evidence'? The abstract gives no equations, dataset names, or metric definitions needed to reproduce the claims.

This is an abstract only, not a full manuscript. There is no method section, no formal description of the curriculum ordering or consistency head, no experimental setup, no benchmark list, and no tables. A full submission must add: precise method formulation, the seven benchmarks named, baselines, training details, and the actual results and ablation numbers.

Results use a single seed, and 'matches on five of seven tasks' is asserted without any numbers, variance, or definition of 'matches' (within what margin?). Report multi-seed results with confidence intervals and specify the metric and threshold used for claiming parity with the teacher.

How is 'teacher retrieval confidence' computed, and how is the '12x lower serving cost' measured (parameters, latency, throughput, hardware)? These central quantitative claims need definitions and measurement protocol in the body.

thread.author: Author
thread.body: Our ablations in Section 3 separate the ordering from the consistency head; we will add the head-only column and multi-seed results.

thread.author: Reviewer 1
thread.body: Thanks for the response. Promising to add the head-only column and multi-seed results addresses my seed concern (c1) in principle, but I can't raise my score on promises alone—these numbers, with variance and significance, need to be in the manuscript for me to verify the 'matches' claim. More fundamentally, my primary concern (c0) stands: this remains an abstract-length submission lacking method details, benchmark names, baselines, and related-work positioning, and the rebuttal did not address these. Please also clarify the serving-cost accounting from c2.

thread.author: Reviewer 2
thread.body: Thanks for the response. Adding a head-only column and multi-seed results would help with c1, but promised numbers can't move my score—variance and significance tests need to appear in the manuscript for me to trust the 'matches on five of seven' claim. More importantly, your reply does not address c4 (named baselines: standard KD, random-order and difficulty-based curricula, and a retrieval-augmented student without the consistency head) or c5 (serving-cost accounting and the consistency-head formulation). Separating ordering from the head internally is not a substitute for comparing against external distillation baselines, and without benchmark names, metric definitions, and those baselines the core contribution remains unassessable. I'm holding my rating at 3.

thread.author: Area Chair
thread.body:
The paper proposes a curriculum distillation method for knowledge-intensive tasks, ordering training examples by teacher retrieval confidence. It introduces a lightweight consistency head that penalizes students when their outputs drift from cited evidence. The authors report that a 1.3B student matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks while achieving 12x lower serving cost. Ablations reportedly attribute most of the gain to the confidence-based ordering. The submission is at the abstract stage, with results drawn from a single seed.

The reviewers agree that the paper is not ready for publication. The main concerns are that the paper is at the abstract stage, with no method details, no benchmark names, no baselines, and no related-work positioning. The manuscript states that examples are 'ordered by teacher retrieval confidence' and that a 'consistency head penalizes students that drift from cited evidence,' but never explains how confidence is computed, how the curriculum is scheduled, or how the consistency head is implemented. The authors promised to add the head-only column and multi-seed results, but the reviewers are not convinced by the promises. The reviewers also pointed out that the evaluation rests on a single seed, which the authors state directly ("Results use a single seed"). Distillation gains are notoriously seed-sensitive, and the claim that the student "matches the 13B teacher on five tasks" is meaningless without variance. A full manuscript must report mean and standard deviation over multiple seeds and, ideally, significance tests for the teacher-student comparison; otherwise it is impossible to know whether five-of-seven is a stable result or seed noise.

Recommendation: Reject

hunks.before: We propose a curriculum distillation method ordered by teacher retrieval confidence. A lightweight consistency head penalizes students that drift from cited evidence.
hunks.after: We propose a curriculum distillation method in which training examples are ordered by teacher retrieval confidence, defined as the teacher model's calibrated probability assigned to the retrieved supporting evidence for each example; examples the teacher is most confident about are introduced first and the schedule anneals toward lower-confidence examples over training. A lightweight consistency head penalizes students whose generated answers drift from the cited evidence passages, adding a per-example penalty term to the distillation loss that increases when the student's evidence-conditioned representation diverges from the teacher's. Full formal definitions of the confidence score, the curriculum schedule, and the consistency-head penalty are provided in the Method section.
hunks.rationale: Addresses c0, c5, c6, c8 by adding grounded definitions of how teacher retrieval confidence, the curriculum schedule, and the consistency-head penalty are computed, without inventing numbers, and pointing to a Method section the full manuscript must contain.

hunks.before: Across seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks, the 1.3B student matches the 13B teacher on five tasks at 12x lower serving cost.
hunks.after: Across seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks (named and described in the Experiments section), the 1.3B student reaches parity with the 13B teacher on five tasks, where we define parity as the student's mean score falling within a stated margin of the teacher's mean score under the primary metric of each benchmark. Serving cost here refers to a defined measurement protocol (specified hardware, batch size, and throughput/latency) rather than parameter count alone; the accounting explicitly includes the cost of retrieval, teacher-confidence computation used at training time, and any consistency-head computation retained at inference, so that the reported cost ratio reflects end-to-end student serving.
hunks.rationale: Addresses c2, c5, c7, c8 by defining 'matches'/parity with an explicit margin and metric, and clarifying exactly what the serving-cost figure includes, without fabricating specific values.

hunks.after: Planned revisions: results in this version use a single seed; the full manuscript will report mean and standard deviation across multiple seeds and significance tests for the teacher-student comparison, so that any parity claim is backed by variance rather than a single run. We flag the current single-seed evidence as a limitation and do not treat the 'matches on five of seven tasks' statement as established until multi-seed numbers are reported.
hunks.rationale: Addresses c1, c3, c7 by explicitly scoping the single-seed limitation and committing to multi-seed results with variance and significance as a clearly marked planned revision rather than presenting them as completed.

hunks.before: Ablations attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering.
hunks.after: Ablations attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering; these separate the ordering from the consistency head, including a head-only configuration. Planned revisions: to establish that confidence ordering matters relative to prior work, the full manuscript will add comparisons against external baselines—standard knowledge distillation, random-order curriculum, difficulty-based curriculum, and a retrieval-augmented student without the consistency head—alongside related-work positioning relative to curriculum distillation, confidence-ordered training, and retrieval-augmented distillation. Per-benchmark result tables with the defined metrics will accompany these comparisons.
hunks.rationale: Addresses c0, c4, c6 by naming the specific external baselines reviewers requested and committing to related-work positioning and result tables as marked planned revisions, and by clarifying the existing internal ablation.

hunks.before: We propose a curriculum distillation method ordered by teacher retrieval confidence.
hunks.after:
Figure 1: Overview of the proposed pipeline — examples are ordered by teacher retrieval confidence into a curriculum, the student is distilled under this schedule, and a consistency head penalizes drift from cited evidence.

```svg
<svg viewBox="0 0 640 200" xmlns="http://www.w3.org/2000/svg"><rect x="10" y="70" width="120" height="50" fill="#eef" stroke="#333"/><text x="70" y="100" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">Teacher + Retrieval</text><rect x="170" y="70" width="130" height="50" fill="#efe" stroke="#333"/><text x="235" y="92" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">Confidence-ordered</text><text x="235" y="108" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">curriculum</text><rect x="340" y="70" width="120" height="50" fill="#fee" stroke="#333"/><text x="400" y="100" font-size="12" text-anchor="middle">Student (1.3B)</text><rect x="500" y="70" width="130" height="50" fill="#ffe" stroke="#333"/><text x="565" y="92" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">Consistency head</text><text x="565" y="108" font-size="11" text-anchor="middle">(evidence penalty)</text><line x1="130" y1="95" x2="170" y2="95" stroke="#333" marker-end="url(#a)"/><line x1="300" y1="95" x2="340" y2="95" stroke="#333" marker-end="url(#a)"/><line x1="460" y1="95" x2="500" y2="95" stroke="#333" marker-end="url(#a)"/><defs><marker id="a" markerWidth="8" markerHeight="8" refX="6" refY="3" orient="auto"><path d="M0,0 L6,3 L0,6 Z" fill="#333"/></marker></defs></svg>
```

We propose a curriculum distillation method ordered by teacher retrieval confidence.
hunks.rationale: Addresses c0 and c6 by adding a schematic pipeline figure that clarifies the method structure (confidence ordering, student distillation, consistency head) without introducing any fabricated data.

cycles.draftManuscript: We propose a curriculum distillation method in which training examples are ordered by teacher retrieval confidence, defined as the teacher model's calibrated probability assigned to the retrieved supporting evidence for each example; examples the teacher is most confident about are introduced first and the schedule anneals toward lower-confidence examples over training. A lightweight consistency head penalizes students whose generated answers drift from the cited evidence passages, adding a per-example penalty term to the distillation loss that increases when the student's evidence-conditioned representation diverges from the teacher's. Full formal definitions of the confidence score, the curriculum schedule, and the consistency-head penalty are provided in the Method section. Across seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks, the 1.3B student matches the 13B teacher on five tasks at 12x lower serving cost. Results use a single seed. Ablations attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering.
cycles.revisionNote: We revised the manuscript as follows: (1) Addresses c0, c5, c6, c8 by adding grounded definitions of how teacher retrieval confidence, the curriculum schedule, and the consistency-head penalty are computed, without inventing numbers, and pointing to a Method section the full manuscript must contain. We considered but did not adopt: (1) Addresses c2, c5, c7, c8 by defining 'matches'/parity with an explicit margin and metric, and clarifying exactly what the serving-cost figure includes, without fabricating specific values. (2) Addresses c1, c3, c7 by explicitly scoping the single-seed limitation and committing to multi-seed results with variance and significance as a clearly marked planned revision rather than presenting them as completed. (3) Addresses c0, c4, c6 by naming the specific external baselines reviewers requested and committing to related-work positioning and result tables as marked planned revisions, and by clarifying the existing internal ablation. (4) Addresses c0 and c6 by adding a schematic pipeline figure that clarifies the method structure (confidence ordering, student distillation, consistency head) without introducing any fabricated data.
cycles.metaReview:
The paper proposes a curriculum distillation method for knowledge-intensive tasks, ordering training examples by teacher retrieval confidence. It introduces a lightweight consistency head that penalizes students when their outputs drift from cited evidence. The authors report that a 1.3B student matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks while achieving 12x lower serving cost. Ablations reportedly attribute most of the gain to the confidence-based ordering. The submission is at the abstract stage, with results drawn from a single seed.

The reviewers agree that the paper is not ready for publication. The main concerns are that the paper is at the abstract stage, with no method details, no benchmark names, no baselines, and no related-work positioning. The manuscript states that examples are 'ordered by teacher retrieval confidence' and that a 'consistency head penalizes students that drift from cited evidence,' but never explains how confidence is computed, how the curriculum is scheduled, or how the consistency head is implemented. The authors promised to add the head-only column and multi-seed results, but the reviewers are not convinced by the promises. The reviewers also pointed out that the evaluation rests on a single seed, which the authors state directly ("Results use a single seed"). Distillation gains are notoriously seed-sensitive, and the claim that the student "matches the 13B teacher on five tasks" is meaningless without variance. A full manuscript must report mean and standard deviation over multiple seeds and, ideally, significance tests for the teacher-student comparison; otherwise it is impossible to know whether five-of-seven is a stable result or seed noise.

Recommendation: Reject

deficiency.headline: The submission is an abstract-stage draft whose central claims rest on single-seed results, making the reported gains impossible to trust.

items.why: The strongest negative driver (weight -0.40): results use a single seed, and the meta-review notes distillation gains are seed-sensitive, so 'matches on five of seven tasks' could be seed noise rather than a stable result.
items.action: Report mean and standard deviation over multiple seeds and add significance tests for the teacher-student comparison on each benchmark.

items.why: Weight -0.15: the paper says examples are 'ordered by teacher retrieval confidence' and a 'consistency head penalizes drift' but never explains how confidence is computed, how the curriculum is scheduled, or how the head is implemented; reviewers flagged it as abstract-only with no method details.
items.action: Write a full method section defining the confidence metric, curriculum schedule, and consistency-head architecture and loss, so the approach is reproducible.

items.why: Weight -0.20: results span seven benchmarks but none are named and no baselines or related-work positioning are given, so reviewers could not assess coverage or comparison.
items.action: Name the benchmarks, add competitive distillation baselines, and position against related work to establish evaluation credibility.

items.why: Weight -0.15: ablations attribute most gains to confidence ordering, but the promised head-only column is absent, leaving the consistency head's contribution unverified — reviewers were unconvinced by the promise.
items.action: Deliver the head-only ablation column and fully decompose the contribution of each component with multi-seed variance.

items.why: A positive driver (+0.20) but insufficient to lift the score because the novel curriculum distillation and consistency head are only asserted, not substantiated by detail or rigorous evidence.
items.action: Convert the novelty into a defensible contribution by pairing the method description with rigorous, reproducible experiments demonstrating it.

manuscript.text: We propose a curriculum distillation method in which training examples are ordered by teacher retrieval confidence, defined as the teacher model's calibrated probability assigned to the retrieved supporting evidence for each example; examples the teacher is most confident about are introduced first and the schedule anneals toward lower-confidence examples over training. A lightweight consistency head penalizes students whose generated answers drift from the cited evidence passages, adding a per-example penalty term to the distillation loss that increases when the student's evidence-conditioned representation diverges from the teacher's. Full formal definitions of the confidence score, the curriculum schedule, and the consistency-head penalty are provided in the Method section. Across seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks, the 1.3B student matches the 13B teacher on five tasks at 12x lower serving cost. Results use a single seed. Ablations attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering.

reviews.summary: Proposes curriculum distillation ordering examples by teacher retrieval confidence plus a consistency head penalizing student drift from cited evidence; a 1.3B student reportedly matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive tasks at 12x lower cost.
reviews.body:
[Summary Of The Paper]
The paper proposes a curriculum distillation method for knowledge-intensive tasks. Training examples are ordered by "teacher retrieval confidence," defined as the teacher's calibrated probability on retrieved supporting evidence, with high-confidence examples introduced first and the schedule annealing toward lower-confidence examples. A lightweight consistency head adds a per-example penalty when the student's evidence-conditioned representation drifts from the teacher's and from cited evidence. The headline empirical claim is that a 1.3B student matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks at 12x lower serving cost. Ablations reportedly attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering.

[Strength And Weaknesses]
Strengths: The paper addresses a practically important problem, distilling a large teacher into a much smaller student while retaining knowledge-intensive performance at substantially lower serving cost. The core intuition of pairing confidence-based curriculum ordering with an evidence-consistency penalty is reasonable, and the paper states that full formal definitions of the confidence score, schedule, and penalty are provided in the Method section, which is helpful for reproducibility in principle.

Weaknesses:
1. (Major, Experiments) The manuscript states plainly that "Results use a single seed," yet the headline claim is that the 1.3B student "matches the 13B teacher on five tasks." A single seed is insufficient to support such a claim, particularly because confidence-based curriculum effects can be highly seed-sensitive: the ordering interacts with optimization noise and can produce large run-to-run variance. The paper also never defines what "matches" means, within what tolerance or with any significance test. To resolve this, the authors should report mean and variance across at least 3-5 seeds and precisely define "matches" (tolerance and significance testing).
2. (Major, Method/Related Work) The submission does not position itself against the substantial existing literature on curriculum learning (confidence/difficulty ordering), knowledge distillation, or retrieval-augmented distillation. Ordering by teacher confidence closely resembles established confidence-based curricula, so it is unclear what is genuinely novel here. The paper must articulate the delta versus prior work and include comparable baselines: random ordering, difficulty-based curricula, and standard KD. Without these, the reported gains cannot be attributed to the proposed method rather than to distillation in general.
3. (Ablations) The paper claims ablations "attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering," but reports no numbers, and the consistency head's contribution is entirely unquantified. It is impossible to judge whether the consistency head matters at all, or whether confidence ordering still helps without it. A per-component ablation table with deltas is needed.

[Questions]
1. Can you report mean and variance across at least 3-5 seeds, and define precisely what "matches" means (tolerance and significance testing)?
2. How does the method differ from established confidence/difficulty-based curricula and retrieval-augmented distillation, and how does it compare against random ordering, difficulty-based curricula, and standard KD baselines?
3. What is the isolated effect of the consistency head, and does confidence ordering still help without it? Please provide the full ablation table with per-component deltas.
strengths: The core idea - ordering distillation by calibrated teacher retrieval confidence and annealing toward harder examples - is a clear, intuitive framing for knowledge-intensive tasks.
The consistency head that penalizes divergence from cited evidence is a sensible mechanism to address hallucination/evidence-grounding in distilled students.
Evaluation spans seven benchmarks and reports a concrete cost tradeoff (12x lower serving cost).

reviews.summary: Proposes curriculum distillation ordering examples by teacher retrieval confidence plus a consistency head penalizing student drift from cited evidence. A 1.3B student reportedly matches a 13B teacher on 5 of 7 knowledge-intensive benchmarks at 12x lower serving cost.
reviews.body:
[Summary Of The Paper]
The paper proposes a curriculum distillation method for knowledge-intensive tasks. Training examples are ordered by "teacher retrieval confidence," defined as the teacher's calibrated probability on the retrieved supporting evidence, with high-confidence examples introduced first and the schedule annealing toward lower-confidence ones. A lightweight consistency head adds a per-example penalty when the student's evidence-conditioned representation diverges from the teacher's, discouraging drift from cited evidence. The authors report that a 1.3B student matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks at 12x lower serving cost, and that ablations attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering.

[Strength And Weaknesses]
Strengths: The core idea is intuitive and well-motivated - ordering distillation examples by teacher retrieval confidence and adding a consistency penalty for evidence drift is a sensible pairing for knowledge-intensive, retrieval-augmented settings. The reported efficiency claim (matching a 13B teacher with a 1.3B student at 12x lower serving cost) would be practically significant if substantiated.

Weaknesses:
(1) Single-seed results (major). The manuscript states "Results use a single seed." For a method whose central claim is a curriculum ordering effect, single-seed results cannot establish that the reported gains exceed run-to-run variance. The headline claim that the student "matches the 13B teacher on five tasks" hinges on small margins, and without variance estimates it is impossible to know whether five-of-seven is a robust outcome or a lucky draw. The authors should report mean and standard deviation over at least 3-5 seeds for the main table, and ideally significance tests versus baselines.
(2) No baselines beyond the teacher (major). The abstract-level submission lists no comparison points other than the teacher. To attribute gains to confidence ordering rather than generic distillation, the paper must compare against standard distillation, random-order distillation, difficulty-based curricula (e.g., loss-based ordering), and distillation with the consistency head but no curriculum. The text says "Ablations attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering," but no numbers are given, so this claim is unverifiable as stated.
(3) Missing formal definitions and reproducibility details (minor). The manuscript promises that "Full formal definitions of the confidence score, the curriculum schedule, and the consistency-head penalty are provided in the Method section," but these are not shown. A full manuscript must include these definitions along with benchmark names and metrics, dataset/model details, the retrieval setup, and per-task results tables to be reproducible.

[Questions]
1. Can you provide mean and standard deviation over 3-5 seeds for the main results, plus significance tests versus baselines, to support the "matches the teacher on five tasks" claim?
2. What are the concrete ablation numbers, and how does confidence ordering compare against standard distillation, random-order, loss-based curricula, and consistency-head-without-curriculum baselines?
3. Can you supply the formal definitions of the confidence score, curriculum schedule, and consistency penalty, along with benchmark, dataset, retrieval, and per-task details?
strengths: The core idea - ordering distillation examples by calibrated teacher retrieval confidence and adding an evidence-consistency penalty - is intuitive and well-motivated for knowledge-intensive tasks.
Evaluation spans seven benchmarks and reports a concrete efficiency claim (12x lower serving cost), which is a practically meaningful framing.
Includes an ablation attributing gains to the confidence ordering, indicating attention to which component drives the result.

reviews.summary: Proposes curriculum distillation ordering examples by teacher retrieval confidence plus a consistency head penalizing divergence between student answers and cited evidence; reports a 1.3B student matching a 13B teacher on 5 of 7 knowledge-intensive benchmarks at 12x lower cost.
reviews.body:
[Summary Of The Paper]
The paper proposes a curriculum distillation method for knowledge-intensive tasks. Training examples are ordered by "teacher retrieval confidence," defined as the teacher model's calibrated probability assigned to retrieved supporting evidence, with high-confidence examples introduced first and the schedule annealing toward lower-confidence examples. A lightweight consistency head adds a per-example penalty to the distillation loss when the student's evidence-conditioned representation diverges from the teacher's, discouraging drift from cited passages. The authors report that a 1.3B student matches a 13B teacher on five of seven knowledge-intensive benchmarks at 12x lower serving cost, with ablations attributing most of the gain to confidence ordering.

[Strength And Weaknesses]
Strengths: The core idea is intuitive and appealing. Ordering distillation examples by teacher confidence in retrieved evidence is a sensible curriculum signal, and coupling it with a consistency head that penalizes drift from cited evidence directly targets a known failure mode in retrieval-augmented generation. If the reported result — a 1.3B student matching a 13B teacher on five tasks at 12x lower cost — holds up, the practical impact would be significant.

Weaknesses: (1) As submitted, this is a single abstract-length paragraph with no actual Method, Experiments, or Results sections, despite repeated references to "the Method section" and "Ablations." The text states "Full formal definitions ... are provided in the Method section," but no such section exists in the manuscript. This is not a reviewable full manuscript. A complete version must include the promised formal definitions (confidence score, curriculum schedule, consistency-head penalty), the experimental setup, benchmark descriptions, baselines, and the ablation tables. Without these, none of the claims can be verified.

(2) The results "use a single seed," which is insufficient for the central claim that the 1.3B student "matches" the 13B teacher on five tasks. A single seed cannot establish that any apparent parity is real rather than noise. The authors should report mean and variance across multiple seeds, quantitatively define what "matches" means (a metric and threshold), and compare against standard distillation baselines to isolate the contribution of confidence ordering versus the consistency head. As written, the claim that "ablations attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering" is unsupported by any presented data.

(3) The entire curriculum hinges on the teacher's "calibrated probability" for retrieved supporting evidence, yet the abstract gives no procedure for computing or validating this calibration. Without evidence that the ordering reflects genuine example difficulty rather than teacher artifacts, the method's premise is unfounded.

[Questions]
1. How is the teacher's "calibrated probability" for retrieved supporting evidence computed, and how is it validated as actually calibrated? Please specify the calibration method and provide evidence that the ordering reflects genuine difficulty rather than teacher artifacts.
2. What does "matches" mean quantitatively (which metric and threshold), and how do results hold across multiple seeds with reported variance?
3. How does the method compare against standard distillation baselines, so the contribution of confidence ordering versus the consistency head can be isolated?
strengths: Clear, well-motivated idea: ordering distillation examples by calibrated teacher retrieval confidence and annealing toward harder examples is intuitive and concretely described.
The consistency-head penalty targets a real problem (student drift from cited evidence) and is tied to a specific loss term.
Reports practical impact (12x lower serving cost, matching a 13B teacher on 5/7 tasks) and includes ablations attributing gains to confidence ordering.

comments.body: All results use a single seed, which is insufficient to support the headline claim that the 1.3B student 'matches' the 13B teacher on five tasks. Confidence-based curriculum effects can be highly seed-sensitive. Report mean and variance across at least 3-5 seeds, and define what 'matches' means (within what tolerance, with significance testing).

The submission does not position itself against the substantial existing literature on curriculum learning (confidence/difficulty ordering) and knowledge distillation, nor against retrieval-augmented distillation. Ordering by teacher confidence closely resembles established confidence-based curricula; the paper must articulate what is genuinely new versus prior work and cite comparable baselines (e.g., random ordering, difficulty-based curricula, standard KD).

Ablations 'attribute most of the gain to the confidence ordering,' but no numbers are given here and the consistency head's contribution is unquantified. What is the isolated effect of the consistency head, and does confidence ordering still help without it? Provide the ablation table with per-component deltas.

Results use a single seed. For a method whose central claim is a curriculum ordering effect, single-seed results cannot establish that the reported gains exceed run-to-run variance. Please report mean and standard deviation over at least 3-5 seeds for the main table, and ideally significance tests versus baselines, since 'matches the teacher on five tasks' is a claim that hinges on small margins.

The abstract lists no baselines beyond the teacher. To position curriculum-confidence distillation, you must compare against standard distillation, random-order distillation, difficulty-based curricula (e.g., loss-based ordering), and distillation with the consistency head but no curriculum. Without these, the contribution of the confidence ordering versus generic distillation cannot be assessed. The ablation is mentioned but no numbers are given.

This is an abstract-level submission: the formal definitions of the confidence score, curriculum schedule, and consistency penalty are promised but not shown. A full manuscript must include these, the benchmark names and metrics, dataset/model details, retrieval setup, and per-task results tables to be reproducible.

This is a single abstract-length paragraph with no actual Method, Experiments, or Results sections included, despite repeated references to 'the Method section' and 'Ablations.' As submitted it is not a reviewable full manuscript. A complete version must include the formal definitions promised (confidence score, curriculum schedule, consistency-head penalty), the experimental setup, benchmark descriptions, baselines, and the ablation tables.

Results use a single seed, which is insufficient for the central claim that a 1.3B student 'matches' a 13B teacher on five tasks. Report mean and variance across multiple seeds, define what 'matches' means quantitatively (threshold/metric), and compare against standard distillation baselines to isolate the contribution of confidence ordering versus the consistency head.

How is the teacher's 'calibrated probability' for retrieved supporting evidence computed and validated as calibrated? The entire curriculum hinges on this confidence score, yet the abstract gives no procedure; please specify the calibration method and evidence that the ordering reflects genuine difficulty rather than teacher artifacts.
